title: Thermometer: sense temperature
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Build a thermometer from a thermistor and a 10 kΩ resistor, upload the Arduino sketch, and read live temperature in Serial Monitor — then pinch the bead and watch the number climb. This is the first sensor whose signal follows a curve, so today you learn how four lines of maths walk one raw reading through resistance and into honest degrees.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the thermometer: a 10 kilo-ohm resistor from 3.3V down to GPIO 1, and a thermistor from that same point to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: Thermistor
parts.explanation: A thermistor changes resistance with temperature. In a voltage divider its changing resistance becomes a changing ADC voltage, and a short formula (the B-equation, with B=3950 for this part) converts that resistance back into degrees. That's the whole thermometer.

parts.name: 10 kΩ resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it always sits in series with a resistor. The 220 Ω value here holds the current gentle enough to light the LED and keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: 3 jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: Feeds the divider from a steady 3.3 volts.
The ADC pin reads the voltage where resistor meets thermistor.
Completes the divider's path back to zero volts.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
The divider's layout is the day's main trap — 10 kΩ to 3.3V, thermistor to GND, and the row where they meet to GPIO 1.
Teach the core idea- this sensor's resistance follows a curve, so the sketch uses the B-equation's logarithm to reach degrees, where earlier analog days rescaled a straight line with map().
Walk the chain out loud using the printed fields — ADC count to voltage, voltage to resistance, resistance to degrees.
A reading near -273 or nan means the divider is open or shorted; check the GPIO 1 midpoint wire and the 10 kΩ resistor before changing code.
If values freeze or ignore a pinch, have the learner reseat the thermistor's legs in their breadboard rows.
Coach the pinch itself — fingers on the bead so body heat transfers; the ADC count and voltage fall as the temperature climbs a few degrees, which is success.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Push the thermistor into the breadboard with its two legs in separate rows.
Place the 10 kΩ resistor so one leg shares a row with one thermistor leg — that shared row is the divider's midpoint.
Run a jumper from 3.3V to the resistor's free leg.
Run a jumper from the midpoint row to GPIO 1.
Run a jumper from the thermistor's free leg to GND.
Compare every wire to the chart, then plug in USB.
Open Sketch_12.1_Thermometer.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Open Serial Monitor and set the baud rate to 115200.
Pinch the thermistor's bead between two fingers and watch the temperature climb.

arduino.explanation: Reads the divider midpoint on GPIO 1 as a number from 0 to 4095.
Works the divider maths backwards to recover the thermistor's resistance in kilo-ohms.
The B-equation — B is 3950 for this bead — maps that resistance onto temperature in Kelvin; subtracting 273.15 lands it in Celsius.

micropython.explanation: Opens the pin to the full 0–3.3 V range so the divider's whole swing is readable.
The same divider arithmetic as the Arduino sketch — volts back into kilo-ohms.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A thermistor's resistance follows a curve as it heats; the divider turns that resistance into a voltage, and the B-equation's logarithm turns the curve back into straight degrees.
theoryModel.formula: ADC count → volts → ohms → degrees — and the last arrow follows a curve, so it takes a logarithm

notes.title: Why this one needs a formula
notes.body: map() draws a straight line between two points, ideal when a sensor rises evenly across its range. A thermistor's resistance follows a curve as it heats, so a straight rescale would match the real temperature at a single point and wander off on either side. The B-equation carries a logarithm that follows the curve, keeping the reading honest from cold to warm.

notes.title: Why resistance moves with heat
notes.body: A thermistor is made of a material whose electrons flow more freely as it warms, so its resistance drops as temperature rises. At 25 °C this bead sits near 10 kΩ, which is why the sketch's reference values are built around 10 and 25.

notes.title: What the 10 kΩ partner does
notes.body: The ADC measures voltage, and the thermistor only offers a changing resistance. Stacking the fixed 10 kΩ resistor above it makes a divider whose midpoint voltage tracks that resistance — the one form the pin can actually read.

test.successCriteria: A steady room line first — somewhere around 20 to 26 °C. Pinch the bead and the temperature climbs a few degrees; let go and it eases back down.

troubleshooting.symptom: Temperature wildly wrong — near -273 or nan
firstChecks: Check the midpoint wire on GPIO 1 and that the 10 kΩ resistor is in the divider.
Reseat the thermistor's legs — one has likely lifted out of its row.
Set Serial Monitor to 115200 baud to match the sketch.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Pinch the thermistor and watch the ADC count and voltage fall while the temperature climbs, then reason back through resistance to explain why all three move the way they do.
